title: A Data-Driven Approach for Mitigating Dark Current Noise and Bad Pixels in Complementary Metal Oxide Semiconductor Cameras for Space-based Telescopes
author: Jia, Peng
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: This is the code for the paper"A Data-Driven Approach for Mitigating Dark Current Noise and Bad Pixels in Complementary Metal Oxide Semiconductor Cameras for Space-based Telescopes". The following is the abstract of the paper.  In recent years, there has been a gradual increment in the performance of Complementary Metal Oxide Semiconductor (CMOS) cameras. These cameras have gained popularity as a viable alternative to charge-coupled device (CCD) cameras in a wide range of applications. One particular application is the CMOS camera installed in small space telescopes. However, the limited power and spatial resources available on satellites present challenges in maintaining ideal observation conditions, including temperature and radiation environment. Consequently, images captured by CMOS cameras are susceptible to issues such as dark current noise and defective pixels. In this paper, we introduce a data-driven framework for the creation of a digital twin for CMOS cameras. Instead of developing a complex simulator and data processing approach, our method includes two primary steps: pixel clustering and polynomial fitting. The pixel clustering method could pick up bad pixels and pixels that have similar dark current noise properties, while the polynomial is crafted according to the Arrhenius law. These two steps together constitute the digital twin. The digital twin leverages ground-based test data to establish distinct temperature-dark current relations for pixels within different clusters. The digital twin could then be utilized to estimate the dark current noise level and detect bad pixels from real observational data. To assess the effectiveness of our approach, we have conducted tests using real observation data obtained from the Yangwang-1 satellite, equipped with a near-ultraviolet telescope and an optical telescope. The results show a considerable improvement in the detection efficiency of space-based telescopes.
